categories.verifiedEligibility: You may qualify if you have a current diagnosed condition, an in-service injury, illness, or event, and a medical link between them, and you were discharged under other than dishonorable conditions.
verifiedSteps: Gather service records, medical records, and a symptom timeline.
If you're still serving with 180 to 90 days left before separation, ask about a Benefits Delivery at Discharge (BDD) pre-discharge claim.
Work with a free accredited VSO or county veteran service officer.
File your claim online at VA.gov or with VSO help.
corrections: No errors found in existing content. The three-part eligibility framing (current condition + in-service event + medical nexus) matches VA's stated requirements at https://www.va.gov/disability/eligibility/.

Verified addition: the Benefits Delivery at Discharge (BDD) filing window is 180 to 90 days before separation, with a requirement to be available for VA exams within 45 days of filing. Source: https://www.va.gov/disability/how-to-file-claim/when-to-file/pre-discharge-claim/

categories.verifiedEligibility: Most veterans who served on active duty and were discharged under other than dishonorable conditions can apply; your priority group depends on service history, disability rating, income, and toxic-exposure status.
verifiedSteps: Apply for VA health care online at VA.gov, by phone at 877-222-8387, or with a VSO.
Check PACT Act toxic-exposure eligibility — many veterans can now enroll directly without first applying for other VA benefits.
Choose a VA facility and schedule an intake appointment.
Ask about mental health and telehealth options.
corrections: Existing content is accurate but omits the PACT Act expansion: veterans who served in Vietnam, the Gulf War, Iraq, Afghanistan, or any post-9/11 combat zone, and veterans exposed to toxins or hazards during training or active duty, can enroll directly in VA health care without first applying for VA benefits. Source: https://www.va.gov/resources/the-pact-act-and-your-va-benefits/
Verified: enrollment is organized into 8 priority groups based on service-connected disability rating, income, and other factors. Source: https://www.va.gov/health-care/eligibility/priority-groups/

categories.verifiedEligibility: Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits scale with qualifying active-duty time — from 50% at 90 days up to 100% at 36 months — and transfers to a spouse or child must be approved by DoD while you're still serving.
verifiedSteps: Apply for benefits and request a Certificate of Eligibility on VA.gov.
If transferring to dependents, request the transfer through DoD while still serving (generally 6 years in with a 4-year additional commitment).
Pick a program and confirm it's VA-approved using the GI Bill Comparison Tool.
Meet the School Certifying Official to enroll.
corrections: The line 'Some benefits are transferable to dependents' understates the conditions: transfer requires at least 6 years of service, an agreement to serve 4 more years, and must be requested and approved through DoD while the member is still in the armed forces — veterans cannot initiate a transfer after separation. A child can generally start using transferred benefits only after the member completes 10 years of service. Source: https://www.va.gov/education/transfer-post-9-11-gi-bill-benefits/

Verified benefit tiers: 90 days-5 months = 50%, 6-17 months = 60%, 18-23 months = 70%, 24-29 months = 80%, 30-35 months = 90%, 36+ months = 100%. Source: https://www.va.gov/resources/how-we-determine-your-percentage-of-post-911-gi-bill-benefits/

categories.verifiedEligibility: You'll need a Certificate of Eligibility based on your service history plus a lender's credit and income approval; a funding fee usually applies unless you're exempt, such as veterans receiving VA disability compensation.
verifiedSteps: Request your Certificate of Eligibility online at VA.gov, through your lender, or by mail (VA Form 26-1880).
Get pre-approved with a VA-experienced lender.
Shop with a real estate agent who knows VA appraisals and the VA loan process.
corrections: No errors found in existing content. Verified funding fee exemptions: veterans receiving VA compensation for a service-connected disability, veterans eligible for compensation but receiving retirement or active-duty pay instead, surviving spouses receiving Dependency and Indemnity Compensation, service members with a proposed or memorandum rating from a pre-discharge claim before closing, and active-duty Purple Heart recipients. Source: https://www.va.gov/housing-assistance/home-loans/funding-fee-and-closing-costs/
Verified: the COE process also confirms funding-fee exemption status, and a funding fee refund may be available if compensation is later awarded retroactive to before closing.

categories.verifiedEligibility: You may qualify with a VA service-connected disability rating of at least 10%, a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, and an employment handicap; a Vocational Rehabilitation Counselor confirms entitlement.
verifiedSteps: Apply for VR&E online at VA.gov (VA Form 28-1900).
Meet with a Vocational Rehabilitation Counselor for an initial evaluation.
Build an approved rehabilitation and employment plan.
corrections: NAMING ERROR: the summary calls the program 'VA Readiness & Employment' — the official name is 'Veteran Readiness and Employment' (VR&E), formerly Vocational Rehabilitation and Employment. Fix the summary text. Source: https://www.va.gov/careers-employment/vocational-rehabilitation/

Verified specificity: eligibility requires a service-connected disability rating of at least 10% (the existing 'typically requires a service-connected disability rating' is correct but can state the 10% minimum). Source: https://www.va.gov/careers-employment/vocational-rehabilitation/eligibility/

Verified addition: the 12-year basic eligibility period applies only to veterans discharged before January 1, 2013; those discharged on or after that date have no time limit. Source: https://www.va.gov/careers-employment/vocational-rehabilitation/eligibility/

categories.verifiedEligibility: Veterans and eligible spouses receive priority of service for DOL-funded job training and placement programs at American Job Centers nationwide, with specialized help for those with significant barriers to employment.
verifiedSteps: Visit or register with your local American Job Center.
Ask about priority of service and JVSG-funded veteran employment specialists (DVOP/LVER staff).
Target veteran-friendly employers, apprenticeships, and registered apprenticeship programs.
corrections: No errors found in existing content. Verified: the Jobs for Veterans Act establishes priority of service for veterans and eligible spouses in DOL-funded job training programs, delivered through roughly 2,600 American Job Centers. Sources: https://www.dol.gov/agencies/eta/jobs-for-veterans-act and https://www.dol.gov/agencies/vets/veterans/veterans-employment-services

Minor precision note: the 'DOL VETS employment representative' step refers to Jobs for Veterans State Grants (JVSG) staff — Disabled Veterans' Outreach Program (DVOP) specialists and Local Veterans' Employment Representatives (LVER) — who are state-employed staff at American Job Centers, not federal DOL employees.

categories.verifiedEligibility: SBA programs like Boots to Business and Veterans Business Outreach Centers serve the military community broadly; SDVOSB/VOSB certification requires a business at least 51% owned and controlled by veterans (service-disabled veterans for SDVOSB), certified through SBA.
verifiedSteps: Take the SBA Boots to Business course (offered through the DoD Transition Assistance Program).
Connect with your local Veterans Business Outreach Center (VBOC).
Apply for SDVOSB/VOSB certification through SBA's Veteran Small Business Certification (VetCert) program at veterans.certify.sba.gov.
corrections: The action step 'Explore SDVOSB/VOSB certification' should specify the current process: certification transferred from VA (CVE) to SBA effective January 1, 2023 under NDAA 2021, and is handled through SBA's VetCert program (veterans.certify.sba.gov), now part of MySBA Certifications (certifications.sba.gov). Sources: https://www.sba.gov/federal-contracting/contracting-assistance-programs/veteran-contracting-assistance-programs and https://veterans.certify.sba.gov/

Verified ownership rule: at least 51% of the business must be owned and controlled by one or more veterans (for SDVOSB, by veterans rated service-disabled by VA). Source: https://www.sba.gov/federal-contracting/contracting-assistance-programs/veteran-contracting-assistance-programs
Verified set-asides: VA sets aside at least 7% of its contracts for certified VOSBs/SDVOSBs; at least 5% of all federal contracting dollars are set aside for certified SDVOSBs.

categories.verifiedEligibility: The Veterans Crisis Line is free, confidential, and available 24/7 to veterans, service members, and their families — no VA enrollment required; dial 988 then press 1, text 838255, or chat online.
verifiedSteps: Save the Veterans Crisis Line: dial 988, then press 1 — or text 838255, or chat online at veteranscrisisline.net.
Ask your VA facility about same-day mental health support.
Connect with a peer support group or Vet Center.
corrections: No errors found. Verified: dial 988 then press 1, text 838255, and 24/7 availability with no requirement to be enrolled in VA benefits or health care; responders are trained in crisis intervention and military culture. Sources: https://www.veteranscrisisline.net/ and https://www.veteranscrisisline.net/about/what-is-988/

Note: veteranscrisisline.net is a .net domain but is the official VA-operated Veterans Crisis Line site, confirmed by VA at https://news.va.gov/105714/new-veterans-crisis-line-number-dial-988-then-press-1/ and https://www.mentalhealth.va.gov/gethelp.asp

categories.verifiedEligibility: Military retired pay (DFAS), VA Veterans Pension, and the Survivor Benefit Plan are separate programs with distinct rules — VA pension is needs-based for wartime veterans who meet age or disability plus income and net-worth limits.
verifiedSteps: Review retirement pay and Survivor Benefit Plan (SBP) elections with DFAS.
Check VA Veterans Pension and Aid & Attendance / Housebound eligibility at VA.gov.
Update beneficiaries and estate documents.
corrections: No errors found. Verified: VA pension, military retirement, and SBP are distinct programs — VA pension is needs-based (income counted against the Maximum Annual Pension Rate, with a net worth limit of $163,699 for Dec 1, 2025 - Nov 30, 2026). Sources: https://www.va.gov/pension/eligibility/ and https://www.va.gov/family-and-caregiver-benefits/survivor-compensation/survivors-pension/rates/

Verified: Aid and Attendance / Housebound benefits are add-ons to VA pension for veterans and survivors who need help with daily activities, are housebound, or are in a nursing home. Source: https://www.va.gov/pension/aid-attendance-housebound/
Note: military retired pay and SBP administration is handled by DFAS (dfas.mil), not VA — the existing 'verify with DFAS, VA' guidance is correct.
